learning_outcomes.description: Apply engineering principles to reduce complex mechanical systems and fluid flows (including the loads and constraints to which they are subjected) to a form that can be analysed using finite element analysis (FEA) or computational fluid dynamics (CFD), respectively.
Derive and solve the system of linear equations that models a truss (and analogous problems in mechanical engineering) or beam using direct and variational approaches.
Select the most appropriate element formulation (truss, beam, membrane, shell, solid) to apply to FEA of mechanical systems.
Model thermal and nonlinear behaviour (contact, plasticity, large deformation) in FEA of mechanical systems using commercial software.

Derive the systems of linear equations that model components (convection, diffusion, convection-diffusion) of the Navier-Stokes equations for incompressible fluid flows using finite difference and finite volume methods.
Model turbulence and heat transfer in fluid flows using commercial CFD software and closure parameters calculated from fluid mechanical theory.
Apply systematic mesh/grid refinement techniques to appropriately discretise (in the context of standard quality metrics) solid/fluid geometries for FEA/CFD using commercial software.

Demonstrate the computational analysis workflow (model definition, dimensional analysis, mesh/grid generation, application of boundary and initial conditions, selection of solution strategy, interrogation of results) in FEA and CFD of mechanical and fluid systems, respectively.
Verify and validate the predictions of FEA and CFD using analytical solutions and experimental data.
Communicate the outcomes of FEA and CFD using appropriate visual aids (e.g. plots, graphs) in formal technical reporting.

assessments.task_description:
Structural analysis of a component or assembly using Siemens NX. This assignment will help demonstrate your ability to (i) develop a practical computational model of an engineering problem of interest, (ii) make sound engineering decisions that facilitate robust, efficient, and accurate analysis, and (iii) interrogate, visualise and document analysis results in a technical letter.

Please refer to Blackboard for detailed marking criteria.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit technical letter (in .pdf format) and analysis file(s) via TurnItIn on Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Computational analysis of a fluid flow problem using Siemens StarCCM+. This assignment will help demonstrate your ability to (i) develop a practical computational model of an engineering problem of interest, (ii) make sound engineering decisions that facilitate robust, efficient, and accurate analysis, and (iii) interrogate, visualise and succinctly discuss analysis results.

Please refer to Blackboard for detailed marking criteria.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

Lecture Series

Lectures on the basic underlying theory of FEA and CFD. Weeks 1-6 will focus on FEA and Weeks 7-13 will focus on CFD.
